chapters.title: Immigrants who built a dynasty
chapters.text:
Early twentieth-century America drew immigrants seeking reinvention. Isaac Sackler left the Austrian Empire; Sophie Greenberg emigrated from Poland. Their marriage launched a medical dynasty as much as a family line. Isaac preached medicine to Arthur, Mortimer, and Raymond, drilling ambition and principle into daily life.

Forbes today ranks the Sacklers America's thirtieth-richest clan.

Eldest son Arthur bore the heaviest expectations and met them early. At fifteen he already covered household expenses. School years doubled as apprenticeship: he edited the paper and shot its photos. Advertising talent surfaced next, spawning side ventures that rivaled his clinical path. Colleagues joked that genius simply followed him room to room.

Keefe's chronicle traces how the brothers would eventually:

• Assemble a global pharmaceutical empire.
• Bankroll museums and universities on a grand scale.
• Become synonymous with opioid catastrophe and public outrage.

chapters.title: Psychiatry's unlikely reformers
chapters.text:
Parental pressure and personal drive sent all three brothers toward medicine despite steep barriers. Arthur finished at NYU; Mortimer and Raymond trained in Scotland because American schools capped Jewish enrollment.

War interrupted Scottish studies, sending the younger two back to a non-accredited Middlesex University campus in Waltham.

Reunion came at Creedmoor Psychiatric Center, where careers began in a specialty medicine barely respected. Mental illness often meant institutionalization and electroshock — standard practice the brothers found barbaric.

They were disgusted at the limitations of their own medical knowledge—at the idea that there was no more humane therapy that they could offer. ~ Patrick Radden Keefe

Research revealed electroshock triggered histamine release, dilating vessels and oxygenating brain tissue. Injectable histamine might substitute for voltage — seed of their chemical-activity hypothesis.

In 1950 they opened the Creedmoor Institute for Psychobiologic Studies, a private lab that catapulted their reputations.

Industry soon produced antipsychotics such as Thorazine, realizing pharmacological psychiatry the brothers had imagined.

Their humane research helped empty overcrowded asylums across the country.

Did you know? 1955 marked the first admissions decline at U.S. mental hospitals in twenty-five years.

chapters.title: Inventing drug marketing
chapters.text:
At his thirtieth birthday, Arthur Sackler had fifteen years of advertising experience, so a part-time job at William Douglas McAdams — an agency specializing in pharmaceutical ads — came easily. His medical expertise, industry grasp, and aptitude for selling helped him rise fast and land prestigious customers like Pfizer.

Pfizer was working on bringing a novel antibiotic named Terramycin to market. Arthur promised to turn Terramycin and Pfizer into household words, and he did. He suggested a new approach: advertising explicitly directed at medical professionals. Overt ads appeared in professional literature, and covert advertisements appeared as papers and studies on treatment efficacy — underwritten by pharmaceutical developers.

Arthur Sackler was the first to conceptualize an idea now known as native advertising.

The campaign was a huge success and revolutionized the industry, guaranteeing Arthur a place in the Medical Advertising Hall of Fame. After two years he was promoted to president and acquired the whole company from McAdams following Terramycin's success.

Soon more pharmaceutical businesses adopted the new distribution strategy. New market relations formed between manufacturers and physicians. Physicians grew more brand-oriented, raising alarm about influence on adequate medical care.

Sackler refuted allegations, claiming his efforts were not advertising but education — assisting colleagues in learning about new treatments flooding the market.

chapters.title: Hidden ownership and the Musketeers
chapters.text:
L. W. Frohlich sometimes overshadowed McAdams by taking big clients who never appeared on Sackler's doorstep. Both men accepted competitive reality and shared the market, yet very few realized they were not adversaries — they were nearly best friends. More intriguing: Sackler was the actual owner of L. W. Frohlich, while Bill Frohlich was the public face. Two competing companies would never hire the same advertising firm, so a new agency was formed with Frohlich as leader. They deceived multimillion-dollar pharmaceutical corporations and became de facto monopolists, hidden because of obvious conflict of interest.

This was not their only hustle. In 1954 Arthur conceived a company aggregating pharmaceutical sales data to sell research to big medical enterprises and improve marketing. Frohlich became official founder and owner.

Arthur's preference for remaining behind the scenes and following gray cardinal policies became a distinguishing characteristic of family business culture.

The most exciting deal for the three Sackler brothers and Bill Frohlich was the Musketeers Agreement merging their enterprises. If one musketeer died, the other three received all his property until the last died; then families received reasonable money while business interests went to charity. Arthur's appetite grew daily, so he resolved not to restrict himself to promoting drugs and tried manufacturing them. In 1952 he acquired small pharmaceutical business Purdue Frederick for his siblings. Shares were evenly distributed, but Arthur remained behind the scenes while Raymond and Mortimer officially ran the business.

chapters.title: Philanthropy as legacy insurance
chapters.text:
Arthur set tone for Sackler commerce and taste. Art collecting began as hobby, then ballooned until private storage felt wasteful. Public patronage promised cultural impact plus personal gain.

Grants purchased entry to elite circles a Brooklyn immigrant's son had once only observed.

Commercial subsidiaries stayed anonymous; charity bore the surname.

Donations also purchased legacy. Names carved into museum marble outlast quarterly earnings. Beneficiaries span continents.

The Washington Post dubbed them modern Medici in 1986 — peak praise for arts funders.

Sample footprint:

• Metropolitan Museum Sackler Wing with Egypt's Temple of Dendur, relocated via Arthur's funding.
• Smithsonian Arthur M. Sackler Gallery.
• Harvard Arthur M. Sackler Museum.
• Guggenheim Sackler Center for Arts Education.
• Oxford Sackler Library.
• Louvre Sackler Wing of Oriental Antiquities.
• British Museum Raymond and Beverly Sackler Rooms.

chapters.title: From MS Contin to OxyContin
chapters.text:
Publicly the brothers looked inseparable; Bill Frohlich's death shattered that image and the Musketeers pact. Domestic and international versions existed, yet Arthur skipped the overseas contract. When Bill died, foreign holdings — including IMS, Arthur's data venture — passed to Raymond and Mortimer, severing ties he valued.

By Arthur's 1987 passing the siblings were estranged. Inheritance battles flared, then eased as elders stepped back and heirs advanced.

Raymond's son Richard, Purdue Frederick CEO, emerged as central successor though shares scattered widely.

The next generation refocused on analgesics, forming Purdue Pharma. MS Contin put morphine inside timed-release tablets while marketing downplayed opioid content.

According to the American Society of Addiction Medicine, more than 80% of heroin users have previously abused prescription opioids.

Campaigns invented a chronic-pain epidemic narrative; advocacy groups — many on Purdue's payroll — insisted prior medicine ignored suffering until pills like MS Contin arrived. Prescribing stayed mostly cancer-related, fortunately.

Patents expire after twenty years, yet regulatory delays shrink exclusive selling windows. Generic rivals loomed.

Mortimer's daughter Katie engineered OxyContin with oxycodone, a semi-synthetic opioid far stronger than morphine, securing the family's next revenue stream.

chapters.title: Profit over the oath
chapters.text:
OxyContin debuted in 1996 as a schedule narcotic under the 1970 Controlled Substances Act despite oxycodone nearing double morphine's potency. FDA reviewer Curtis Wright's favorable ruling — later tied to Sackler influence — opened retail prescribing.

Legions of reps flooded clinics with distorted safety claims, targeting any prescriber they could reach. Monthly revenue climbed into billions while addiction rates surged nationwide.

Late-nineties alarms triggered a five-year federal probe through millions of corporate documents.

Purdue pleaded guilty to felony misbranding. Executives Friedman, Goldenheim, and Udell absorbed probation plus community service; the firm paid six hundred million dollars. No Sackler name surfaced in indictments or headlines — anonymity by design.

Staff were coached to shield family members mafia-style, rewarding loyalty above transparency.

Fines changed little; marketing continued despite regulator warnings.

chapters.title: Collapse without accountability
chapters.text:
Purdue's main remedy for misuse was a crush-resistant tablet — a move that extended patent life as much as it claimed to protect patients.

The reformulation backfired. Users unable to crush pills frequently migrated to heroin, deepening the epidemic. The family insisted abusers, not manufacturers, bore responsibility.

Media investigations soon mapped the crisis to corporate origins. A Los Angeles Times report debunked the twelve-hour pain-relief promise, documenting withdrawal cravings when doses wore off early — clarifying why dependence spread so fast. Lawsuits accumulated; forty-nine states sued by 2020. For the first time, Sackler family members faced courts directly, yet ties within the Trump administration and top-tier lawyers blunted punishment.

Before bankruptcy in 2022, the family drained Purdue Pharma's remaining value and withdrew.

Settlements will cost billions, but imprisonment appears unlikely and personal wealth largely endured.

Ironically, they offered roughly twelve billion dollars to resolve claims and convert Purdue into an addiction charity — reviving the musketeers' long-ago philanthropic vision.

chapters.title: A name erased
chapters.text:
Isaac reminded his sons that a good name is the richest inheritance a father can leave. Arthur, Mortimer, and Raymond engraved Sackler on walls of elite scientific, educational, and artistic institutions. Through the twentieth century the surname signaled psychiatric advances, bold medical advertising, and generous cultural giving.

A new generation in the twenty-first century attached darker meanings: dangerous pills, opioid deaths, deceptive marketing, hidden facts, court battles, bankruptcy, and alleged bribes. Each member denied personal wrongdoing, yet museums and universities began removing the name from facades and galleries.

Money could not buy the immortality Arthur craved through philanthropy; scandal accomplished what marble could not — erasure from public honor.

Try this

Given America's opioid emergency, take prescribing seriously. Seek credentialed specialists, verify drug information online, and purchase only through authorized pharmacies.
